back: Establishes that there shall be a President of India, creating the head of the Indian State.
Prescribes the procedure for the impeachment of the President on the ground of 'Violation of the Constitution'.
Grants pardoning power to the President (pardon, reprieve, respite, remission, or commutation) for Union offenses, court-martial sentences, and death sentences.
Mandates that the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.
Provides for the appointment and duties of the Attorney General for India, the highest law officer in the country.
Provides for a Joint Sitting of both Houses of Parliament to resolve deadlocks over Ordinary and Financial Bills.
Defines a Money Bill, certified exclusively by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha.
Mandates the presentation of the 'Annual Financial Statement' (commonly known as the Budget) before Parliament.
Empowers the President to promulgate Ordinances during the recess of Parliament.
First citizen and head of state; elected by elected MPs and state MLAs; minimum age is 35 years.
Ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha; elected by all MPs (elected + nominated) of both Houses.
Real executive head (de facto); appointed by the President; acts as the primary channel of communication between the Cabinet and the President.
Elected by Lok Sabha members; presides over Lok Sabha and Joint Sittings; certifies Money Bills; resigns to the Deputy Speaker.
Appointed by the President; holds office during the pleasure of the President; has the right to attend and speak in Parliament without voting.
Introduced only in Lok Sabha with prior Presidential recommendation; Rajya Sabha can delay it for a maximum of 14 days without amendments.
Can originate in either House; Rajya Sabha can delay it for up to 6 months; deadlocks are resolved via Joint Sitting (Article 108).
Contains Article 110 matters plus general legislation; introduced only in Lok Sabha with Presidential recommendation, but Rajya Sabha enjoys full voting powers.
Governed by Article 368; requires special majority in both Houses; no provision for Joint Sitting; President must give assent.
Consists of 22 members (15 LS + 7 RS); examines CAG reports; chaired traditionally by an Opposition member.
Largest parliamentary committee with 30 members, ALL drawn exclusively from Lok Sabha; examines government expenditure estimates.
Consists of 22 members (15 LS + 7 RS); examines reports and accounts of Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs).
The minimum number of members required to transact business in a House; set at 1/10th of the total membership (Article 100).
The first hour of a parliamentary sitting devoted to asking and answering Starred, Unstarred, and Short Notice Questions.
An informal Indian innovation starting right after Question Hour (~12 PM) to raise urgent matters without prior notice.
Termination of a session of Parliament by the President; does not cause pending bills to lapse.
Terminating a sitting of Parliament without naming a day for reassembly; executed by the Presiding Officer.
Capped the total number of Ministers (including the PM) at 15% of the total strength of the Lok Sabha.
Reduced the voting age for Lok Sabha and Assembly elections from 21 years to 18 years (amended Article 326).
Discontinued the nomination of 2 Anglo-Indian members to the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies.
6 months and 6 weeks (6 months max interval between sessions + 6 weeks after reassembly).
